plainMeaning: We bow to the Jain community pictured as a mighty wheel — its hub restraint and austerity, its rim right-faith — and pray it stay forever victorious.
recitation: saṁjama tava tuṁbāragassa, namo sammatta pāriyallassa | appaḍi cakkassa jao, hoi sayā saṅgha cakkassa ||

wordByWord.etymology: Skt saṁyama, from saṁ- + √yam 'to hold/restrain'; first member of the hub-compound.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; here it forms the wheel's central nave together with tapa.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt tapas, from √tap 'to heat, to burn away (karma)'.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; paired with saṁyama as the driving core of the wheel.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tuṁba (Skt tumba, the gourd-shaped nave/boss of a wheel) + āra/āraga (Skt ara 'spoke'); genitive sg -assa = Skt -asya.
wordByWord.notes: tumba literally 'gourd' (so the rounded nave); the compound saṁyama-tapa-tuṁbāraga makes restraint and austerity the hub and spokes.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt namaḥ, from √nam 'to bow'; governs the following genitives.
wordByWord.notes: namo + genitive ('homage to …') frames the whole wheel-description.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sammatta = Skt samyaktva, from samyañc 'right, correct'.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; here imaged as the wheel's encircling rim.

wordByWord.gloss: of the felly / rim (the encircling band of the wheel)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pāriyalla, with pari- 'around, encircling'; genitive sg -assa.
wordByWord.notes: Lexically rare; read here per the wheel-imagery as the nemi (rim) that binds and rolls — see notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt a-prati, privative a- + prati 'against, counter'; bound to cakka.
wordByWord.notes: appaḍi-cakka = Skt apraticakra, 'a wheel against which no rival wheel can stand' — echoing a cakravartin's invincible discus.
First occurrence, qualified by appaḍi: 'the irresistible wheel'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jao = Skt jaya, from √ji 'to conquer'.
Prakrit hoi = Skt bhavati, 3rd sg present of √bhū 'to be'.
wordByWord.notes: Benedictive force with sayā: 'may there always be'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt saṅgha 'assembly'; forms the tatpuruṣa saṅgha-cakka.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; the object praised throughout the hymn.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt cakra; genitive sg -assa; in compound saṅgha-cakkassa = 'of the Saṅgha-wheel'.
wordByWord.notes: Repeated to close the verse, naming the image outright.

literalTranslation: To [the wheel] whose nave-and-spokes are restraint and austerity, homage — to [the wheel] whose rim is right-faith; victory be always to the irresistible wheel, to the Saṅgha-wheel.
idiomaticTranslation: Homage to the wheel whose hub and spokes are restraint and austerity, whose encircling rim is pure right-faith! May victory be forever to that unopposable wheel — the wheel that is the Saṅgha.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Moving from the static fortified city of the previous verse to a thing that turns and conquers, the poet now casts the community as a wheel (cakka). The image is engineered with care. A wheel transmits force only when three parts cohere: a solid nave, sound spokes, and a rim that grips the ground. Here the nave-and-spokes (tuṁbāraga) are saṁyama and tapa — restraint and austerity, the inner discipline that bears the load and drives motion; the encircling rim (pāriyalla) is sammatta, right-faith, the band that holds the whole together and alone touches the world. Faith on the circumference, conduct at the centre: the wheel rolls forward only when both are intact. The poet then names it appaḍi-cakka, the 'irresistible wheel' — Sanskrit apraticakra, a wheel that meets no answering wheel. The resonance is the cakravartin's divine discus (cakra-ratna), the world-conqueror's weapon no rival can withstand; the Saṅgha is the spiritual sovereign's invincible wheel, and behind it glints the dharma-cakra, the turning of the teaching. The verse is not description but benediction: namo (homage) opens it and jao hoi sayā — 'may victory be always' — closes it. The advance to the next verse's war-chariot is already prepared: this is the second of the martial similes, the community imagined not merely as a stronghold but as a power in motion, ever-rolling, ever-winning. The whole gāthā hangs on a single grammatical hinge — namo governing a chain of genitives (tuṁbāragassa … pāriyallassa … cakkassa … saṅgha-cakkassa), so that every clause bows to one and the same wheel.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse quietly stages the ratna-traya (three jewels). Right-faith (samyag-darśana) is placed not alongside but around restraint and austerity — as the rim encircles the hub. This is precise Jain doctrine: without samyaktva, ascetic practice is mere bodily affliction (akāma-nirjarā) that spins without conquering; faith is what makes tapa and saṁyama soteriologically effective, binding conduct into a wheel that actually rolls toward liberation. The epithet apraticakra asserts the invincibility of that disciplined, faith-girt community over rival doctrine — victory not by force but by the integrity of restraint, austerity, and correct insight working as one mechanism.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this within the Pākṣika svādhyāya, the practitioner is invited to check that their own austerity and restraint are 'rimmed' by right-faith rather than spinning on ego or mere habit; only then does practice carry one forward. The closing 'jao hoi sayā saṅgha-cakkassa' is chanted as an active blessing of victory upon the whole four-fold community to which the reciter belongs.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 1.1 — samyag-darśana-jñāna-cāritrāṇi mokṣamārgaḥ (the three jewels as the path)
Cakravartin's cakra-ratna as the invincible discus, source of the apraticakra epithet
Nandī-sūtra opening Saṅgha-stuti (the chain of Saṅgha similes)

sources.title: Tumba (Sanskrit/Prakrit dictionary entry — 'gourd', incl. the rounded nave of a wheel)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of tumba underlying tuṁbāraga (the gourd-shaped nave/hub of a wheel)

sources.title: Cakra (multi-tradition dictionary entry — wheel/discus)
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of cakka/cakra and the discus/wheel imagery of appaḍi-cakka and saṅgha-cakka

sources.title: Samyaktva (Jain dictionary entry — right-faith)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of sammatta = samyaktva, the wheel's rim

sources.title: Saṃyama (Jain/Sanskrit dictionary entry — self-restraint)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of saṁjama = saṁyama as monastic restraint

sources.title: Jaya (dictionary entry — victory)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of jao = jaya and the benedictive 'victory be always'

notes: The wheel-reading (hub = saṁyama+tapa, rim = right-faith, an irresistible Saṅgha-wheel) follows the authoritative structure tradition and the verse's own grammar (namo governing genitives ending -assa). 'pāriyalla' is lexically rare; it is taken here as the felly/rim (nemi) of the wheel on the strength of the encircling pari- and the established hub-rim imagery — a commentarial, not strictly dictionary-attested, gloss, so flagged as somewhat uncertain. 'tuṁbāraga' is parsed as tumba (gourd-shaped nave) + āra (spoke); some recensions may read the second member differently. Note: several open-web AI 'translations' of this gāthā invert its sense (reading appaḍi as 'just as' or treating the verse as condemning those fallen from the Saṅgha); these are spurious and were not followed. Verse text, anusvāras and conjuncts are kept verbatim per the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha book; minor mātrā/spelling variants across Nandī recensions are possible.
